coredata.dc:title: Enhanced capacitive energy storage of polyetherimide at high temperatures by integration of electrical insulation and thermal conductivity
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Central South University. Publishing services by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of KeAi Communications Co. Ltdé

coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiaona
Luo, Hang
Zhai, Di
Wan, Yuting
He, Guanghu
Hu, Deng
Hou, Hongshuai
Zhang, Dou
Zhang, Shujun
coredata.dc:description: Polymer dielectrics possessing excellent electrical insulation and high thermal conductivity are pivotal for dielectric capacitors at elevated temperatures. However, the integration of electrical insulation and thermal conductivity in polymers remains a challenge. In this work, we present a feasible strategy to integrate high electrical insulation and high thermal conductivity by bonding carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the diamine monomer of polyetherimide (PEI). The CQDs with Coulomb blockade effect serve as traps for the migrating of electrons in the dielectrics, while the bonding networks formed by CQDs and PEI further deepen the traps and augment trap density. As a result, the hybrid dielectrics (PEI-NH2-CQDs) exhibit nearly an order of magnitude higher electrical resistivity than that of pure PEI, leading to an 80% increase in discharge energy density with an energy efficiency of 90% at 200 ​°C compared to pure counterpart. Additionally, this all-organic dielectric achieves a significantly increased thermal conductivity of 0.65 ​W ​m−1 ​K−1 compared to 0.26 ​W ​m−1 ​K−1 of PEI, which supports its cyclic stability at elevated temperatures. We also demonstrate the kilogram-scale production of CQDs, synthesizing over 8 ​kg in a single batch, paving the way for large-scale production of reliable PEI-NH2-CQDs dielectrics.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dielectrics
Energy storage
Carbon quantum dots
High temperatures
Thermal conductivity

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Kilogram image of CQDs; (b) TEM, high-resolution TEM image, and size distribution of CQDs; (c) Schematic of PEI-NH2-CQDs; (d) Preparation process of PEI-NH2-CQDs.

(a) FTIR spectrum within 3000∼4000 ​nm of PEI-NH2-CQDs with different masses of CQDs; (b,c) High-resolution N 1s of (b) PEI and (c) 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs; (d) Plots of absorbance versus time, the insert is an optical image of (d1) PEI and (d2) 5 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs on the 3rd day; (e) The interchain spacing and Young's modulus of samples; (f) Glass transition temperature and DSC curves of PEI-NH2-CQDs with different masses of CQDs.

(a) Resistivity of PEI-NH2-CQDs with different CQDs content at 150 and 200 ​°C; (b) Leakage current density vs electric field curves fitting the hopping conduction mechanism and (c) Weibull breakdown strength of PEI-NH2-CQDs with different CQDs contents at 150 and 200 ​°C; (d) Energy storage performance at 200 ​°C.

Comparison of PEI, PEI-NH2-CQDs, and PEI-CQDs. (a) Weibull breakdown strength; (b) Energy storage performance; (c) Fitting of leakage current density vs electric field curves based on hopping conduction; (d) The trap energy level and trap density obtained by TSDC.
(a) Schematic of Coulomb blockade effect; (b) The value of the capacitance (C) vs the distance d; (c,d) Schematic of the polymer chain and electronic states in (c) pure PEI and (d) PEI-NH2-CQDs.

(a) Infrared images of PEI and PEI-NH2-CQDs during the synchronous heating process; (b,c) Steady-state temperature distribution of (b) pure PEI and (c) PEI-NH2-CQDs; (d) Cyclic performance of PEI and PEI-NH2-CQDs at 200 ​°C and 200 ​kV ​mm−1; (e) Power density and energy efficiency of different regions in PEI-NH2-CQDs with 17×13 ​cm2 size; (f) Fast charge-discharge test of BOPP and PEI-NH2-CQDs.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under the responsibility of Central South University.

head.ce:title: Enhanced capacitive energy storage of polyetherimide at high temperatures by integration of electrical insulation and thermal conductivity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Powder Metallurgy, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Superconducting and Electronic Materials, Faculty of Engineering and Information Sciences, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, NSW 2522, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute for Superconducting and Electronic Materials
Faculty of Engineering and Information Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute for Superconducting and Electronic Materials, Faculty of Engineering and Information Sciences, University of Wollongong, Wollongong, NSW 2522, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Polymer dielectrics possessing excellent electrical insulation and high thermal conductivity are pivotal for dielectric capacitors at elevated temperatures. However, the integration of electrical insulation and thermal conductivity in polymers remains a challenge. In this work, we present a feasible strategy to integrate high electrical insulation and high thermal conductivity by bonding carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with the diamine monomer of polyetherimide (PEI). The CQDs with Coulomb blockade effect serve as traps for the migrating of electrons in the dielectrics, while the bonding networks formed by CQDs and PEI further deepen the traps and augment trap density. As a result, the hybrid dielectrics (PEI-NH2-CQDs) exhibit nearly an order of magnitude higher electrical resistivity than that of pure PEI, leading to an 80% increase in discharge energy density with an energy efficiency of 90% at 200 ​°C compared to pure counterpart. Additionally, this all-organic dielectric achieves a significantly increased thermal conductivity of 0.65 ​W ​m−1 ​K−1 compared to 0.26 ​W ​m−1 ​K−1 of PEI, which supports its cyclic stability at elevated temperatures. We also demonstrate the kilogram-scale production of CQDs, synthesizing over 8 ​kg in a single batch, paving the way for large-scale production of reliable PEI-NH2-CQDs dielectrics.

This work introduces an innovative molecular engineering approach to integrate high electrical insulation and high thermal conductivity in hybrid polyetherimide-based dielectrics, that is, to bond carbon quantum dots exhibiting a Coulomb blockade effect with the diamine monomer of polyetherimide. The hybrid dielectrics presents a superior comprehensive performance compared with pure PEI.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Polymer electrostatic capacitors play a pivotal role in advanced power electronic systems due to their exceptional properties, including high voltage resistance, rapid charge-discharge rate, mechanical flexibility, and reliable performance [1–7]. The growing demand from emerging fields such as hybrid vehicles, oil and gas exploitation, and aerospace power systems escalates the need for polymer dielectrics capable of high operating temperatures exceeding 150 ​°C [8–11]. However, commercial dielectric biaxial-oriented polypropylene (BOPP) suffers from low energy storage density and limited operating temperature (<105 ​°C) [12,13]. To meet the demand for high temperatures, cooling systems are often incorporated, adding bulk and weight to devices and restricting efforts toward miniaturization [14–17]. Therefore, developing polymers with enhanced high-temperature resistance is desirable for boosting the practical application of dielectrics.

Recently, polyetherimide (PEI) has attracted widespread attention due to its high glass transition temperature (T g≈217 ​°C) and low dielectric loss [18,19]. Unfortunately, the leakage current of this aromatic polymer increases dramatically at elevated temperatures, leading to an increased conduction loss [20]. High conduction loss not only reduces discharge energy efficiency (η) and energy density (U e) but also generates substantial Joule heat which poses a significant risk to the lifespan of the capacitive components. Therefore, the integration of high electrical insulation and thermal conductivity is of vital importance for high-temperature dielectrics [21].

A variety of modification methods have been applied to improve the electrical insulation of PEI-based dielectrics, including composite system construction and crosslinked structure implementation [22–24]. For instance, the incorporation of inorganic fillers with wide band gaps and organic semiconductor molecules with high electron affinity contribute to the formation of deep traps, which appreciably suppresses the conduction loss and improves breakdown strength (E b) [25–30]. However, the thermal conductivity in these studies is neglected. Inorganic boron nitride nanosheets (BNNSs) with high thermal conductivity are commonly used to raise the thermal conductivity of polymer dielectrics [14,31,32]. Liu et al. [33] designed a crosslinked PEI-based dielectrics tightly coated on the surface of BNNS, realizing an improved thermal conductivity of around 0.5 ​W ​m−1 ​K−1 at room temperature. Although the incorporation of BNNS is an effective method to improve thermal conductivity, the poor compatibility with polymers leads to unsatisfactory electrical insulation performance. The integration of high electrical resistance and high thermal conductivity is challenging to achieve in a dielectric. Therefore, it is a task of top priority to create a novel strategy with the dual objective of improving high electrical insulation and thermal conductivity.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are defined as cost-effective zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial composed of easily dispersed nanoparticles with diameters less than 10 ​nm, showcasing the quantum effect [34–36]. Even at elevated temperatures, the ultra-small size of CQDs enables the Coulomb blockade effect, where the entry of a single electron into a CQD impedes the passage of subsequent electrons. Previous studies have incorporated CQDs as fillers into PEI-based dielectric, in which the leakage current was significantly suppressed due to the Coulomb blockade effect [37,38]. However, the surface functional groups of CQDs have not been utilized, and the thermal conductivity of these dielectrics has been neglected. In this work, we synthesized organic fillers CQDs using a simple aldol condensation method at the kilogram scale. CQDs serve as deep traps capable of capturing electrons, causing the trajectory of free electrons to become more tortuous, thereby delaying dielectric breakdown. Moreover, the abundant carboxyl functional groups (-COOH) on the surface of CQDs react with terminal amino groups (-NH2) of PEI, forming a crosslinked network. In this case, CQDs function as central junction points in hybrid dielectrics (PEI-NH2-CQDs), improving thermal stability, reinforcing mechanical strength, and deepening the electron traps while increasing trap density. Therefore, nearly an order of magnitude higher electrical resistivity than that of pure PEI is achieved in PEI-NH2-CQDs, resulting in a high U e of 3.6 ​J ​cm−3 with η of 90% at 200 ​°C. Moreover, due to the increase in the heat dissipation pathway formed by the bonding network, PEI-NH2-CQDs exhibit a high thermal conductivity of 0.65 ​W ​m−1 ​K−1, which is 2.5 times higher than that of pure PEI. The integration of high electrical insulation and thermal conductivity is realized in PEI-NH2-CQDs hybrid dielectrics. Additionally, the cost-effective film preparation involves the incorporation of CQDs without producing by-products, highlighting the practical potential of this approach.

After the aldol condensation reaction, the achieved polymer chains with multiple functional groups and tiny carbon clusters entangle to form carbon quantum dots (CQDs), on the kilogram-scale production. As shown in Fig. 1 a, 8.166 ​kg CQDs were fabricated within 2 ​h. Fig. 1b shows the TEM image exhibiting the morphology of the achieved CQDs. From the results of size distribution, CQDs have diameters ranging approximately from 1 to 4 ​nm with the majority of particles falling within the size range of 1.5–3 ​nm. The image reveals no particle agglomeration, indicating that the CQDs are easily dispersed. The crosslinked strategy further assures the dispersibility of CQDs. Through the reaction between the -COOH groups on the surface of CQDs and the terminal -NH2 groups of PEI, individual CQD is positioned at the ends of polymer chains. The long PEI chains effectively prevent the aggregation of CQDs. The XRD pattern (Fig. S1) shows a broad diffraction peak at 2θ of 17.7°, revealing the amorphous structure of CQDs. Further analysis with FTIR and high-resolution C 1s spectrum (Figs. S2 and S4) confirms the presence of the oxygen-containing functional group, such as -COOH, on the surface of CQDs, revealing that CQDs are amorphous nanoclusters with abundant -COOH functional groups [39–41]. As illustrated in Fig. 1c and 1d, the pre-polymerization of PEI monomers (BPADA and MPD with excess MPD) forms polymer chains terminated with -NH2 groups, which can react with -COOH groups. The abundant -COOH groups on the surface of CQDs facilitate multiple reactions between the -COOH groups of a CQD and -NH2 groups of different polymer chains, resulting in the formation of a crosslinked structure. This hybrid polymer is designated as PEI-NH2-CQDs. For comparison, pure polymer without CQDs is labeled as PEI, and composites containing CQDs without crosslined network is labeled as PEI-CQDs.

The resulting films are characterized, and the cross-sectional SEM image is shown in Fig. S5, revealing a uniform and dense morphology with a thickness of 9∼10 μm. The FTIR and XPS results confirm that the CQDs successfully react with -NH2 groups of the PEI chains. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a and Fig. S6, the FTIR spectrum exhibits a diminished peak at 3376 ​cm−1 corresponding to -NH2 groups [42], confirming an increase in the number of reacted -NH2 groups with higher CQDs content. XPS results of PEI and PEI-NH2-CQDs are presented in Fig. S7, and the mass percentage of elements is given in Table S1. Due to the introduction of CQDs containing only carbon and oxygen elements, the mass percentage of nitrogen in polymers decreases from 4.1 ​wt% to 3.4 ​wt%. In the polymer chains, nitrogen exists predominantly in two states: N-H and C-N [43]. As illustrated in the high-resolution N 1s spectrum in Fig. 2b and c, the ratio of SN-H/SC-N decreases from 0.29 for PEI to 0.22 for 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs, further demonstrating that partial -NH2 groups have been converted to amide bonds.

It should be noted that crosslinked polymers are hardly soluble in solvents, so the absorbance of the solution can reflect the degree of crosslinking [44]. Films of identical quality are immersed in the same volume of NMP solvent, and the dissolution processes are recorded (Fig. S8). After three days, the PEI film exhibited curly and tiny filamentous shapes (Fig. 2d1), whereas the 5 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs film remained largely intact (Fig. 2d2). The absorbance of the clear liquid (Fig. 2d) decreases with increasing CQDs content, indicating a higher degree of crosslinking for PEI-NH2-CQDs with higher CQDs content. The bonding structure changes molecular chain interactions, as evidenced by the coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE). As shown in Fig. S9, PEI-NH2-CQDs exhibit a reduced CTE of 22 ​ppm ​°C−1 compared to PEI (25 ​ppm ​°C−1), indicating a denser structure in PEI-NH2-CQD [45]. This finding is further supported by the XRD pattern (Fig. S10). Fig. 2e shows the interchain spacing derived from the XRD pattern using Bragg's equation. As expected, the interchain spacing decreases from 4.284 ​Å for PEI to 4.226 ​Å for 5 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs. The denser structure in PEI-NH2-CQDs decreases the free volume within the material [46]. Mechanical performance is also improved. The Young's modulus increases steadily with the increase of CQDs content, highlighting the strengthening effect of crosslinking. As illustrated in Fig. 2f the glass transition temperature (T g) of hybrid polymers increases, from 216.26 ​°C for pure PEI to 226.39 ​°C for 5 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs. This enhancement can be attributed to the cross-linking structure, which restricts the movement of molecular chains, thereby improving the thermal stability of the polymers. For comparison, PEI-CQDs are prepared and characterized for T g, as depicted in Fig. S12, showing a lower T g than that of the polymer PEI-NH2-CQDs, verifying the benefit of crosslinked structure which improves thermal stability.

The dielectric properties of hybrid polymers as a function of frequency and temperature are characterized, as depicted in Fig. S13. The introduction of CQDs has a marginal effect on the dielectric constant (ε r) and dielectric loss (tanδ). Notably, the tanδ remains low (<0.005) even at elevated temperatures, contributing to a high discharged energy efficiency. Fig. 3 a exhibits the electrical resistivity of dielectrics at elevated temperatures, where the electrical resistivity of hybrid polymers is nearly an order of magnitude higher than that of pure PEI. At 150 ​°C and 200 ​°C, the 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs performs the highest electrical resistivity of 3.07×1013 and 2.46×1011 ​Ω ​m, respectively, which are more than one order of magnitude higher than that of pure PEI, with electrical resistivity being on the order of 8.33×1012 and 3.02×1010 ​Ω ​m. Dielectrics with high electrical resistivity correspond to low conduction loss, especially at elevated temperatures [42].

The leakage current density under varying electric fields was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3b and Fig. S14a, the leakage current behavior follows the hopping conduction. With the improvement in crosslinking degree, the leakage current and hopping distance (λ) initially decrease and then increase. This trend occurs because denser chain packing in PEI-NH2-CQDs decreases the free volume within the material, leading to a reduction in the average free path of migrating carriers. Consequently, this suppresses the rapid movement of charge carriers [46]. The 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs exhibit the lowest leakage current density, corresponding to the smallest λ, i.e., 0.99 ​nm at 150 ​°C and 1.09 ​nm at 200 ​°C. As the crosslinking degree continues to increase, the molecular chain segments tend to curl and entangle, leading to the formation of defects within the film. This, in turn, results in an increase in λ. Correspondingly, the electric field deviating from Ohm's law to exponential field-dependent conduction (E tr) increases from 47 ​kV ​mm−1 for PEI-NH2 to 83 ​kV ​mm−1 for 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs, as shown in Fig. S14b. A higher E tr suggests higher conductivity activation energy (W a) and deeper trap depth, signifying that electron migration is less active in 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs [47]. Compared to commercial polymers (Fig. S15), 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs exhibit the smallest λ and the highest E tr, which favorably inhibits electron migration and enhances the E b.

Weibull distribution analysis of samples tested at elevated temperatures (Fig. 3c and Fig. S16) reveals that the E b initially increases and then decreases with increasing CQDs content, reaching a peak value of 600 ​kV ​mm−1 at 150 ​°C and 530 ​kV ​mm−1 at 200 ​°C for 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs. The initially increased E b is attributed to the deeper trap formation and increased Young's modulus with increasing crosslinking degree. The defects formed by excess crosslinking may cause the decrease of E b in the 5 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs. According to the displacement-electric field (D-E) curves (Figs. S17 and S18), the energy storage performance, including U e and η, is evaluated. As illustrated in Fig. 3d and Fig. S19, the U e of PEI-NH2-CQDs is higher than that of PEI. Specifically, at an elevated temperature of 200 ​°C, U e with η exceeding 90% (U 90) is 2.0 ​J ​cm−3 for PEI, reaching the maximum value of 3.6 ​J ​cm−3 for 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs, and then slightly decreases to 3.4 ​J ​cm−3 for 5 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs. Thanks to the lowest leakage current density and highest breakdown strength, the 3 ​wt% PEI-NH2-CQDs sample achieves the optimized energy storage performance, outperforming other commercial polymers, as witnessed in Fig. S20.

To highlight the impact of the crosslinked network of CQDs on energy storage performance, the electrical characteristics of the PEI-NH2-CQDs with crosslinked network, PEI-CQDs without crosslinked network, and pure PEI were compared. The frequency-dependent and temperature-dependent dielectric properties shown in Fig. S21 reveal minimal difference in dielectric constant and dielectric loss between the PEI-NH2-CQDs and PEI-CQDs samples. Fig. 4 a and b depict the Weibull distribution and energy storage performance at elevated temperatures. PEI-CQDs, with E b value of 510 ​kV ​mm−1 and U 90 value of 3.3 ​J ​cm−3 at 200 ​°C, exhibit significantly higher capacitive performance compared to PEI but inferior to PEI-NH2-CQDs. Leakage current density analysis, as evidenced in Fig. 4c, indicates that the hopping distance of PEI-CQDs is 1.27 ​nm, smaller than that of PEI but larger than that of PEI-NH2-CQDs. These results suggest that the incorporation of CQDs merely as fillers can increase trap density, while adding CQDs as bonding sites further enhances trap density by forming a crosslinked network. Further analysis using thermal-induced depolarization current curves (Fig. S25) reveals the trap energy levels (E TSDC) and trapped charge quantity (Q TSDC), as shown in Fig. 4d. PEI-NH2-CQDs exhibit an E TSDC of 5.3 ​eV and a Q TSDC of 1.89 ​nC, while PEI-CQDs show a significantly lower E TSDC of 0.6eV and Q TSDC of 0.62 ​nC. This finding showcases that the PEI-NH2-CQDs possess deeper trap depth and higher trap density. Collectively, these results highlight the effect of CQDs, used as crosslinked sites, in enhancing the capacitive energy storage performance of PEI-NH2-CQDs.

To understand the improved performance in PEI-NH2-CQDs, the underlying mechanism is investigated. When a free electron enters a quantum dot, Coulomb repulsion among electrons generates a charging energy of e2/2C, where e is the electronic charge, C is the capacitance between two particles [48]. To satisfy the Coulomb blockade effect, it is necessary to meet the condition e2/2C>k B T, where k B is Boltzmann's constant and T is temperature [48,49]. This implies that C<3.11×10−18 ​F at RT and C<1.96×10−18 ​F at 200 ​°C, respectively. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, when a free electron (α) enters an unoccupied CQD, Coulomb repulsion causes the subsequent electron (β) to deviate from its original migration path β2 to a new path β3. This deviation causes the trajectory of free electrons to be more tortuous, thereby extending the breakdown pathways. To verify the Coulomb blocking effect in CQDs, a simple model is employed to estimate the C of CQDs according to the equation [50]:
(1)
C = 2 π
ε 0
ε r
d 2
− 4
r 2
∑
j = 0
∞
cot
h
j +
1 2
arccos
h
d
2 r
− 1
where ε 0 and ε r are vacuum dielectric constant and relative dielectric constant of PEI, respectively, r is the radius of CQDs (measured to be less than 5 ​nm according to TEM images), d is the distance between electrode and CQD, as illustrated in Fig. 5b.

The results, illustrated in Fig. 5b, indicate that although the value of C increases with the increasing radius of CQDs, it remains within the range satisfying the Coulomb blockade effect, confirming that CQDs in the PEI matrix exhibit Coulomb blockade effect. In addition, the electron affinity (E A) of CQDs was tested by Cyclic voltammogram, as shown in Fig. S26a. The onset oxidation potential (E red) is −0.56 ​eV. According to the equation E LUMO=−(E red+4.4), where E LUMO is the lowest occupied molecular orbital, the E LUMO of CQDs is −3.84 ​eV. A deep trap is constructed due to the difference in electron affinity between PEI (2.78 ​eV) [51] and CQDs (3.84 ​eV), as shown in Fig. S26b. As illustrated in Fig. 5c and d, the CQDs serve as traps to capture free electrons and create a more tortuous migration path for unbound electrons. The crosslinked structure leads to denser chain stacking, which further deepens traps and increases trap density, effectively hindering electron transport. The molecular engineering strategy significantly improves the electrical insulation performance of the hybrid PEI-NH2-CQDs.

Thermal stability and reliability are critical considerations for practical dielectric capacitor applications. During operation, heat buildup can raise the internal temperature of capacitors, potentially exceeding the T g [52]. To mitigate this, the thermal conductivity (K) of polymers plays a crucial role in promoting efficient heat diffusion, which can be calculated by equation (2),
(2)
K = ρ ∙ α ∙
C p
where ρ is the density of polymers, α is thermal diffusivity and C p is specific heat capacity. Surprisingly, the K of PEI-NH2-CQDs is 0.65 ​W ​m−1 ​K−1, which is 2.5 times higher than that of pure PEI and 2.2 times higher than that of PEI-CQDs, as compared in Table S2. The thermal conductivity of amorphous polymers mainly relies on the propagation of phonons in bonding interactions (covalent bonds). The improved K is attributed to the contribution of new bonding interactions between molecular chains in crosslinked dielectrics, which provide additional propagation paths for phonons [53,54]. The enhanced heat dissipation capability of polymers can be intuitively demonstrated through a straightforward temperature measurement setup, as illustrated in Fig. S27.

Using PEI as a diffuser, the average temperature of the ceramic heating plate rises to 121 ​°C and >150 ​°C after 30 ​s and 60 ​s, respectively, as shown in Fig. 6 a. In contrast, the average temperatures of a ceramic heating plate with PEI-NH2-CQDs as diffuser are much lower, 103 ​°C after 30 ​s and 146 ​°C after 60 ​s. The superior high thermal conductivity can be further validated through a steady-state distribution simulation, using finite element calculations that account for the thermal conductivity and capacitor geometry (Fig. S28). As shown in Fig. 6b and c and Fig. S29, the peak internal temperature of PEI-NH2-CQDs under an electric field of 200 ​kV ​mm−1 is much lower than that of PEI and PEI-CQDs. This difference is due to the higher K and lower electrical conductivity of the hybrid materials, which suggests that PEI-NH2-CQDs capacitors are highly suitable for high-temperature applications. Consequently, PEI-NH2-CQDs showcase superior high-temperature cycling reliability compared to pure PEI. While pure PEI can only withstand 2510 cycles, PEI-NH2-CQDs maintain stable operation for up to 63 ​100 cycles, as displayed in Fig. 6d. Moreover, the capacitive performance and thermal conductivity of this work are compared with other reported dielectrics, as shown in Table S3. In contrast to reported studies that achieve either high energy density or high thermal conductivity—properties typically exhibiting an inverse correlation—this work successfully breaks this trade-off relationship. Unlike previous studies about quantum dots, which primarily emphasize energy density, this work successfully integrates considerable energy density and high thermal conductivity.

To evaluate the efficacy of the large-scale production, a large film of 17×13 ​cm2 was fabricated and divided into 9 regions to characterize its homogeneity. As illustrated in Fig. 6e, the values of U e and η across different regions remained consistent at 200 ​°C and 200 ​kV ​mm−1, validating the high uniformity of PEI-NH2-CQDs dielectric. Additionally, under the same conditions of 200 ​°C and 200 ​kV ​mm−1, a fast discharge time of 2.76 μs and a high discharge power density of 0.22 ​MW ​cm−3 were achieved in PEI-NH2-CQDs (Fig. 6f). This discharge power density outperforms that of commercial BOPP (0.13 ​MW ​cm−3) tested at 120 ​°C. These results underscore the strong potential of PEI-NH2-CQDs for practical applications.

We successfully fabricated kilogram-scale CQDs and utilized them to prepare hybrid dielectrics PEI-NH2-CQDs by an innovative molecular engineering approach. The CQDs are incorporated as bonding sites to form a crosslinked network, combining the Coloumb blockade, to effectively trap free electrons and extend the trajectory of unbound electron movement. As a result, the hybrid dielectrics PEI-NH2-CQDs demonstrate a significantly improved electrical resistivity of 2.46×1011 ​Ω ​m at 200 ​°C. An improved energy density of 3.6 ​J ​cm−3 with a discharged energy efficiency exceeding 90% is achieved at 200 ​°C. Additionally, the high thermal conductivity of 0.65 ​W ​m−1 ​K−1 for PEI-NH2-CQDs contributes to the superior thermal stability and reliability of the capacitors, reinforcing the potential of this dielectric in the field of high-temperature energy storage applications. All in all, the integration of electrical insulation and thermal conductivity is realized in hybrid dielectrics PEI-NH2-CQDs.

Sodium hydroxide (NaOH), acetaldehyde 40% aqueous solution and hydrogen chloride (HCl) solution (1 ​M) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd. 1,3-phenylenediamine (MPD) and 4,4’-(4,4′-isopropyldiphenyloxy)diphthalic anhydride (BPADA) were sourced from TCI. 1-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, A.R., >99.0%) was purchased from Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co, China. If not specified, all materials were used directly without additional processing.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100F (Japan), while scanning electron microscope (SEM) was conducted using a TESCAN MIRA4 LMH. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained using a Nicolet iS50 within a wavenumber of 500–4000 ​cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was carried out using a Panalytical Empyrean with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.5418 ​Å), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on an AXIS Supra+. Cyclic voltammetry measurements were performed on a CHI660E electrochemical workstation using a three-electrode system, with platinum electrodes serving as both the working electrode and counter electrode, and a silver electrode as the reference electrode. UV–Vis spectrophotometer was recorded with a UV-2600i. The coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE) test was performed at a heating rate of 3 ​°C min−1 with a preload of 0.02 ​N on a TS Instrument TMA450. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was performed at a heating rate of 10 ​°C min−1 using a TA Instrument DSC 25. Nano-indentation tests were conducted with a Poisson's ratio of 0.3 using an Ultra Nanoindentation Tester UNHT.

Au electrodes with a diameter of 3 ​mm were sputtered on both sides of the films for electrical characterization. Thermal-induced depolarization current (TSDC) was recorded with a Keithley 6517B electrometer. The samples were polarized for 30 ​min under a DC electric field of 50 ​kV ​mm−1 at 230 ​°C, and then quickly cooled to −30 ​°C for 10 ​min with the electric field on. After removing the electric field, the samples were short-circuited. Finally, heat the samples to 250 ​°C at a heating rate of 3 ​°C min−1 and record the current. Frequency-dependent dielectric properties were tested using an E4990A impedance analyzer over a frequency of 100–107 ​Hz. Temperature-dependent dielectric properties were measured using a dielectric temperature spectrum measurement system (BALAB, DMS-500). Electrical resistivity was recorded by a TH2690A Insulation Resistance Meter. Electric displacement-electric field (D-E) loops, leakage current, and cyclic charge-discharge testes were measured at 100 ​Hz using a TF Analyzer 3000 series. Fast charge-discharge tests were conducted with a resistor of 10 ​kΩ using a DCQ-20A (PolyK Technologies). Thermal diffusivity was characterized on a LFA 467 HyperFlash. The density of polymers was obtained by an Archimedes method using the electronic balance. Specific heat capacity was measured with a TA Q20 differential scanning calorimeter (DSC).

The steady-state temperature distribution simulation was calculated on COMSOL Multiphysics. The capacitor encloser size was set to 40×40×10 ​mm3 and the capacitor interior was wrapped with 6-μm-thickness dielectric film metallized with 40-nm-thickness aluminium. For every sample element with a length of Δx, width of w, surface area of S and cross-sectional area of A, the heat in and out balance can be expressed as:
− K A
d T
d x
| x
+ K A
d T
d x
|
x + Δ x
− h S
(
T −
T ∞
)
− ε σ S
(
T 4
−
T ∞ 4
)
= 0
, where K is the thermal conductivity, T ∞ is environmental temperature, h is the convection heat transfer coefficient (air free convection is 35 Wm-2 K−1), ε is the emissivity, and σ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant [14].

To prepare CQDs, 8 ​kg of NaOH was slowly added into 40 ​L of acetaldehyde solution and the mixture was stirred at ambient temperature. The resulting products were obtained by centrifugation and subsequently washed with dilute hydrochloric acid and deionized water. Finally, CQDs were dried at 70 ​°C for 12 ​h under a vacuum environment.

The hybrid polymers were fabricated by a simple solution casting method. The precursor PAA-NH2 was synthesized by blending BPADA and MPD at a mole ratio of 1:1.02. 0.6 ​mmol of BPADA and 0.612 ​mmol of MPD were sequentially added to 2.5 ​ml of NMP with a half-hour interval and stirred for 7 ​h until the reaction was complete. The excess MPD ensured that PEI was terminated with amino groups. Then, a certain mass of CQDs was introduced into the mixture and stirred continuously for 10 ​h. Subsequently, the solution was cast onto a clean glass slide. The cast film was heated at 80 ​°C for 2 ​h, 100 ​°C for 1 ​h, 150 ​°C for 1 ​h, 200 ​°C for 1 ​h, 250 ​°C for 1 ​h, and 300 ​°C for 1 ​h, respectively. At last, the film was peeled off the glass sheet by soaking in deionized water and the peeled film was heated at 70 ​°C for 12 ​h under a vacuum environment to evaporate the water.
The preparation process of uncross-linked polymer PEI-CQDs was identical to that of PEI-NH2-CQDs, with the only difference being the mole ratio of BPADA and MPD, which was 1:1.

Xiaona Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Hang Luo: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Di Zhai: Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization. Yuting Wan: Visualization, Investigation, Conceptualization. Guanghu He: Visualization, Conceptualization. Deng Hu: Visualization, Conceptualization. Hongshuai Hou: Methodology, Conceptualization. Dou Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Shujun Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (52172265), Excellent Youth Science Foundation of Hunan Province (2022JJ20067), The Science and Technology Innovation Program of Hunan Province (2022RC1074), Central South University Innovation-Driven Research Program (2023CXQD010).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effect of crosslink formation on heat conduction in amorphous polymers
J. Appl. Phys.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Kikugawa, T. G. Desai, P. Keblinski, T. Ohara, Effect of crosslink formation on heat conduction in amorphous polymers, J. Appl. Phys. 114(3) (2013) 034302.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A molecular dynamics study on heat conduction of crosslinked epoxy resin based thermal interface materials for thermal management
Comp. Mater. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Liu, Z. Rao, A molecular dynamics study on heat conduction of crosslinked epoxy resin based thermal interface materials for thermal management, Comp. Mater. Sci. 172 (2020) 109298.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiaona Li is a Ph.D. candidate at the Powder Metallurgy Research Institute of Central South University, where she is currently researching polymer-based dielectrics for energy storage applications. Her primary focus is on investigating the influence and mechanisms of chain structure and filler properties in polyimide-based dielectrics.

Hang Luo received his Ph.D. degree from the Powder Metallurgy Research Institute of Central South University (CSU) in 2016 and then worked as a post-doctor at the School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, CSU. He is currently a Professor at the Powder Metallurgy Research Institute of Central South University. His research is focused on interface design, polymer-based dielectrics for energy storage and ferro-/piezo-electric ceramics. He has published more than 60 papers in international journals.

Dou Zhang obtained his Ph.D. degree in Metallurgy & Materials from the University of Birmingham. He is currently a professor at the Powder Metallurgy Research Institute of Central South University and leads a well-established and multi-disciplinary research group. His research is focused on ferroelectric ceramics and films for energy and microwave applications; piezoelectric ceramics and composites sensors and actuators for a range of smart applications; porous ceramics; bioceramics; ceramic microsystem techniques, including colloidal micro molding and 3D printing. Particular attention is paid to the controllability of fine-scale structures and their impacts on the devices' performance.

Shujun Zhang is a Distinguished Professor at University of Wollongong, Australia, prior to which, he served as a Senior Scientist and Professor at the Pennsylvania State University, USA. Presently, his focus lies in exploring the intricate relationship between design & fabrication, microstructure, properties, and device performance of electronic materials, particularly with applications in piezoelectric transducers and energy storage/harvesting. He has received several accolades, including an Academician of World Academy of Ceramics, an IEEE Fellow of the Ultrasonics, Ferroelectric and Frequency Control Society (UFFC-S); a Fellow of the American Ceramic Society. Additionally, he is a recipient of IEEE UFFC-S Ferroelectrics Recognition Award; a winner of the New South Wales Premier's Prizes for Science & Engineering; the Ross Coffin Purdy Award at the American Ceramic Society; and Vice-Chancellor's Research Excellence Award for Researcher of the Year at UOW. He is the Editor-in-Chief for Microstructures, section Editor-in-Chief for Crystals, as well as an Associate Editor for the IEEE Transactions on UFFC, Journal of the American Ceramic Society, and Science Bulletin.
